narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in competitive fields like this. The admissions model evaluated your profile, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, LSAT score of 31, Year 1 grade decile of 5, Year 3 decile of 3, upper-middle family income, and plan to attend full-time. It predicted a 73% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—schools use this to prioritize applicants likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 grade decile, which ranks lower than typical students' and signaled potential struggles in sustaining strong academic performance. Your LSAT score, notably below the average of around 37, was the next biggest contributor, as lower scores often correlate with bar exam difficulties in the model's view. Your undergraduate GPA, also under the usual 3.21 average, added to this concern by suggesting room for improvement in handling rigorous coursework. Even your upper-middle family income played a small role in nudging the prediction toward failure, perhaps reflecting patterns in the data where such backgrounds don't always translate to bar success without top academics. On a brighter note, your full-time enrollment intention and Year 1 decile helped slightly, pulling the prediction toward passing since most students are full-time and solid early performers fare better. Compared to typical applicants, your academics dipped below averages across key metrics, which outweighed these positives for the model. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar hurdles through targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your GPA with postgraduate courses, and seeking mentorship programs tailored for Black women in law to build a standout reapplication. You're capable of turning this around; keep pushing, and reapply with a fortified profile. We've seen stories like yours lead to success.
